    you need data available in OneLake for a Fabric workload and must decide whether to **virtualize it in place (shortcut)** or **bring a copy in (ingest)**. Observable entry terms: the data either already exists in an accessible lake (another OneLake item/workspace/tenant, ADLS/S3/GCS) or in an external operational DB, and you have not yet committed to copying.
  • treePower BI storage mode — Direct Lake vs Import vs DirectQuery
    you are choosing the **storage mode** for a Power BI semantic model over Fabric data, and must commit before building. Observable entry terms: the data is in OneLake (Lakehouse/Warehouse Delta) or an external source; you are weighing freshness vs query speed vs refresh cost. If Direct Lake is the answer, you **also** name the variant (on-OneLake vs on-SQL) — that is a separate, non-optional decision.
Best practices 21
  • Pattern — explicit DAX measures (and gold-side precomputation) are the strong default; calculated columns for aggregatable values are an anti-pattern that bloats the model and, on Direct Lake, isn't even available.Aggregate with measures, not calculated columns — push the math to gold or to DAX measures
  • Pattern — medallion is the strong default; skipping a layer or smearing responsibilities across layers needs a written reason.Bronze raw, silver curated, gold business-ready — keep the layer boundaries
  • Pattern — the store-selection decision tree is the strong default; picking a store by habit (or by the presence of the word "SQL" in the ask) is the documented anti-pattern.Choose lakehouse vs warehouse from the decision tree, not from the word "SQL"
  • Pattern — Dataflow Gen2 **Fast Copy** for extract-load and Spark/notebooks for heavy reshaping is the strong default; pushing complex transforms through the standard mashup engine is the slow path.Fast Copy to land, Spark to reshape — don't make the mashup engine do heavy transforms
  • Pattern — explicit, ETL-aware framing is the strong default for any model fed by a multi-step pipeline; rely on automatic updates only when freshness-on-every-commit is genuinely wanted.Frame deliberately — control when Direct Lake sees new data, don't let "auto" surprise you
  • Pattern — workload isolation plus surge protection is the strong default for any capacity mixing BI with background jobs; "one capacity for everything" is the documented anti-pattern (house opinion #5).Isolate noisy workloads; use surge protection — one capacity for everything starves the interactive users
  • Pattern — Liquid Clustering / Z-order is the strong default for silver/gold file skipping; static partitioning is reserved for a low-cardinality, high-volume case with a written reason.Liquid Clustering over static partitioning — and never high-cardinality partition columns
  • Pattern — incremental, idempotent loads with explicit watermark/control-table state are the strong default for pipeline-based ingestion; full reloads on every run are an anti-pattern outside small/initial loads.Make ingestion incremental and idempotent — own the watermark, don't reload the world
  • Pattern — a star schema (conformed dimensions + fact tables) is the strong default for any Direct Lake / Import semantic model; a single wide flat gold table is an anti-pattern outside narrow exceptions.Model a star schema, not one wide flat table — VertiPaq and DAX both reward it
  • Absolute rule — "Direct Lake" without the mode is an unfinished sentence, and the omission changes the fallback behavior you design for.Name your Direct Lake mode — on-OneLake or on-SQL
  • Primary diagnostic — when a Delta table is slow or a Direct Lake model errors after maintenance, check the OPTIMIZE/VACUUM cadence first. The 7-day VACUUM floor is an **absolute rule**.OPTIMIZE to compact, VACUUM to reclaim — and never VACUUM below a framed Direct Lake commit
  • Absolute rule (no hand-editing prod) / Pattern (parameterize stage-specific bindings) — hand-editing a prod workspace is a bug; relying on autobind for Direct Lake / data-source connections is the documented gotcha.Promote dev→test→prod through deployment pipelines, and parameterize every per-stage binding
  • Pattern — strong default; copy only with a written reason. (Calling Mirroring "free" without the caveat is an outright bug.)Reach for a OneLake shortcut before copying data
  • Absolute rule — serving bronze to Direct Lake / the SQL endpoint is a bug; the gold-table optimization targets are the strong default.Shape gold for Direct Lake; never serve bronze
  • Pattern — transforming on ingest (update policies) and pre-aggregating (materialized views) is the strong default in an Eventhouse; reshaping the same raw stream on every query is the slow, expensive path.Shape streaming data at ingest with update policies and materialized views — don't reshape at query time
  • Pattern — sizing to average-plus-smoothing is the strong default; sizing for peak is the documented over-provisioning anti-pattern (house opinion #5).Size capacity to smoothed average, not peak — let the 24-hour smoothing window do its job
  • Absolute rule (on-OneLake side) / Primary diagnostic — exceeding the per-SKU guardrails either silently degrades to DirectQuery (on-SQL) or errors/fails framing (on-OneLake); the failure shape is mode-specific and must be designed for.Stay under the Direct Lake guardrails — or know exactly how your mode fails when you don't
  • Pattern — Eventhouse/KQL DB is the strong default for data-in-motion (streaming, telemetry, logs, time-series); streaming into a lakehouse Delta table is the documented wrong-store anti-pattern (house opinion #2).Streaming and telemetry belong in an Eventhouse — don't force real-time into a lakehouse Delta table
  • Primary diagnostic — when a Direct Lake model goes cold (slow) after every data load despite small changes, check whether the gold load pattern is destructive first.Update gold non-destructively — destructive rewrites kill incremental framing
  • Pattern — V-Order on gold consumed by Direct Lake / SQL is the strong default; V-Order on bronze (or on write-heavy staging) is a write tax with no reader to pay it back.V-Order earns its keep on read-heavy gold — pay the write tax only where reads cash it in
  • Absolute rule (the two-plane model) / Pattern (workspace-per-layer-and-stage topology) — treating workspace roles as data access, or promising RLS/CLS on an engine where it's preview, is a bug (house opinion #6).Workspaces and domains are the governance boundary — security at the right plane, not the wrong role
microsoft-graph12 trees · 18 practices
Decision trees 12
  • treeGraph API — "what changed?" (poll vs delta vs change-notification)
    You need to react to changes in a Graph resource (new mail, updated user, new Teams message) and are deciding how to detect them — not a one-time read.
  • treeGraph API — advanced query (default vs ConsistencyLevel=eventual)
    You need `$count`, `$search`, `$orderby` with `$filter`, or `$filter` on a directory property that the default query engine rejects (often a `400` telling you to use advanced query).
  • treeGraph API — request efficiency (single vs page-loop vs $batch)
    You are issuing one or more Graph reads/writes and deciding how to structure the calls to minimize round-trips and avoid throttling.
  • treeGraph API — throttled (HTTP 429) response
    A Graph call returned **429 Too Many Requests** (or you are designing for it). Observable: `429` status + a `Retry-After` header.
  • treeGraph directory — which group type
    Creating a group via Graph and choosing the type by what it must do (security gating vs collaboration vs automatic membership).
  • treeGraph files — small upload vs upload session
    Uploading a file to OneDrive/SharePoint via Graph and choosing the upload mechanism by size.
  • treeGraph identity — app credential type
    A confidential client (web app, daemon, API) needs a credential to prove itself to Entra. Choosing between client secret, certificate, and managed identity.
  • treeGraph identity — delegated vs application permission
    You are choosing the permission type for an app calling Graph and must decide between **delegated** (acts as a signed-in user) and **application** (acts as itself, no user).
  • treeGraph identity — user vs admin consent
    Deciding whether a permission can be granted by an end user or requires a tenant administrator. Observable: the permission's consent requirement and whether the resource is org-wide.
  • treeGraph identity — which auth flow
    You've chosen the permission type and need the OAuth2 flow for token acquisition. Observable: the client type (SPA, web app, daemon, mobile/CLI, API-calling-Graph-for-a-user).
  • treeGraph Teams — which permission for messages
    Reading or posting Teams chat/channel messages and choosing the permission model. Observable: whether a user is present and whether the app is installed in the team.
  • treeGraph workloads — change detection (delta vs change-notification subscription)
    You must react to changes in a workload resource (mailbox, drive, group membership, Teams channel) and are choosing between delta polling and a push subscription.
Best practices 18
  • Primary diagnostic — when a directory-object `$count`/`$search`/`$orderby`/`ne`/`endsWith` query errors or silently ignores `$count`, the advanced-query headers are missing.Advanced query — ConsistencyLevel: eventual + $count, deliberately
  • Pattern — when you have several independent Graph calls, `$batch` them; deviate only with a reason.Batch to cut round-trips — combine independent calls into one $batch
  • PatternCache tokens with MSAL; don't mint a token per call
  • Absolute ruleCertificates (or managed identity), not client secrets, in production
  • Absolute rule — picking delegated or application by convenience instead of by "is there a signed-in user?" is a security defect, not a style preference.Delegated vs application permissions is a design choice — state it before writing auth code
  • Pattern — for "what changed since last time," use a delta query or change notification, not a periodic full read.Delta for what changed — track changes, don't re-pull the collection
  • PatternDesign for the lowest consent tier the scenario allows
  • Absolute rule — ignoring `Retry-After` or hammering after a `429` makes throttling worse, not better.Honor throttling as a contract — wait Retry-After, then back off with jitter
  • Absolute rule — a `.ReadWrite.All` / `.All` permission where a narrower one would do is an over-privilege defect, and the verdict is a security control.Least-privilege permission selection — request the narrowest scope that works
  • Absolute rule — code that reads `value` once and stops has shipped a silent sampling bug.Page to exhaustion — a first page is not "all results"
  • Absolute rule — the client type dictates the flow; using the wrong one is a security or correctness defect (implicit and ROPC are off the menu).Pick the OAuth2 flow by client type — not by habit
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason and a routed verdict.Resource-scoped permissions over tenant-wide `.All` — when a scoped grant exists, the `.All` is over-privilege
  • Absolute rule — the decryption private key for rich notifications is a security control; it lives in Key Vault and its handling escalates to security review, never decided silently.Rich notifications carry encrypted payloads — verify the signature and guard the private key
  • Absolute rule — a collection `GET` with no `$select` is an over-fetch bug, not a style choice.Select only what you need — never read a Graph collection bare
  • Absolute rule — a change-notification subscription with no renewal timer and no lifecycle handling is a silent outage, not a working integration.Subscriptions are stateful and expire — renew before expiry and handle lifecycle notifications
  • PatternUse the Graph SDK, not raw HTTP, for resilience
  • Absolute rule — shipping `/beta` to production is a risk decision someone must own out loud, never a silent default.v1.0 not /beta in production — beta is unversioned and can break without notice
  • Absolute rule — a webhook endpoint that skips the validation handshake never receives a subscription, and one that processes notifications without verifying the sender is an injection surface.Validate the notificationUrl handshake, then verify every notification's sender
power-platform34 trees · 58 practices
Decision trees 34
  • treeALM tooling — Power Platform Pipelines vs Azure DevOps vs GitHub Actions
    You're standing up (or restructuring) the promotion pipeline for a solution and must choose the orchestration tool — before anyone exports a `.zip`.
  • treeApp type — canvas vs model-driven vs code app
    you are starting a new Power Apps build (or re-platforming an existing one) and must pick the app *type* before any screen/form work. Observable triggers: "build an app for <process>", "should this be canvas or model-driven", "we need a custom React app on the platform". Not for choosing a UI control *inside* an app (that's the surface tree below).
  • treeApp UI surface — PCF vs web resource vs canvas component vs Custom Page
    you need a custom UI element *inside* an app (not a whole new app) and must pick the cheapest surface that delivers it. Observable triggers: "build a custom control for <X>", "the form needs a control the built-ins don't have", "should this be a PCF". For *which React technology* a PCF/code-app uses, defer to the React-surface tree in [`pcf-react-fluent-platform-libraries.md`](pcf-react-fluent-platform-libraries.md).
  • treeAutomation surface — cloud flow vs desktop flow (RPA)
    You must automate an interaction with some system and are deciding *how* the flow touches it. Observable input: does the target system expose a programmatic surface (connector / REST / OData / SOAP), or is its UI the only way in?
  • treeCanvas data access — delegation-safe vs accept-the-cap
    you are writing or reviewing a canvas `Filter`/`LookUp`/`Search`/`Sort` against a connector and must decide whether the query is safe. Observable triggers: the blue "delegation" underline in Studio, a "query can't be delegated" warning, or a report that "the app shows the wrong/missing data" once a table grew past a few hundred rows.
  • treeCopilot agents — which builder?
  • treeCopilot Studio — Grounding source choice (where the AI step lives)
    An agent needs an AI/answer step and you must choose where it runs — Copilot Studio generative answers, AI Builder prompts, a custom prompt-flow, or direct Azure OpenAI. Observable trigger: "the bot needs to answer from our docs" or "we need an LLM step here" and you're weighing cost/governance/control.
  • treeCopilot Studio — Topic vs generative answers
    Deciding, for a specific intent in a Copilot Studio agent, whether to author a deterministic topic or let generative answers handle it. Observable trigger: building a new intent, or a deployed bot is hallucinating / giving inconsistent answers on a class of questions.
  • treeData modeling — addressing rows across environments
    code/flows/imports need to reference a Dataverse row in a way that **survives moving between dev/test/prod**, where the GUID differs. Observable trigger: you're about to hard-code a GUID, or designing a data-migration script. Decisive axis: **is there a natural unique key**, and does the data **already contain duplicates**.
  • treeData modeling — cascade behavior on a 1:N relationship
    you're configuring `CascadeConfiguration` on a 1:N relationship and must set Delete / Share / Assign / Reparent. Observable trigger: creating the relationship payload, or the child table is (or will be) high-volume. Decisive axis: **child row count per parent** and whether children have **standalone meaning**.
  • treeData modeling — choice vs lookup vs customer column
    you're adding a column that "picks one value from a set" and must choose the type. Observable trigger: a single-select field where you're unsure between an option set and a lookup. Decisive axes: **who owns/maintains the value list** and **do you need polymorphism (Account ∪ Contact)**. Remember column **data type is permanent**.
  • treeDataverse Web API — get a bearer token
    you need to call `https://<org>.api.<region>.dynamics.com/api/data/v9.2/…`
  • treeDerived values — rollup vs calculated vs plug-in vs flow
    you need to **store/show a derived value** (a total, a concatenation, a status) and must pick the mechanism. Observable trigger: "how do I keep this parent total / computed field up to date?" Decisive axes: **same-record vs child-aggregate**, and **real-time vs eventual**.
  • treeDLP precedence — which policy governs THIS app/flow?
    A flow/app is being evaluated against DLP and you need to predict which policy actually governs it — typically while diagnosing "why did my flow get suspended" or "why is this connector allowed here but not there."
  • treeDLP — classifying a connector (Business / Non-Business / Blocked)
    A connector needs a DLP classification — a maker requested it, a custom connector was added, or you're authoring the tenant policy and must place a connector in exactly one bucket.
  • treeDoes THIS environment need Managed Environments turned on?
    Deciding whether to enable Managed Environments (Environment management) on a specific environment — weighing the proactive governance benefit against the premium-per-user licensing it obliges.
  • treeEnvironment topology — single, multi-stage, or per-project?
    You're designing how many environments a workload (or a tenant) needs, and how to carve them — observable drivers are number of users, data confidentiality, monetary/reputational impact, and ALM need.
  • treeError handling — which resilience pattern?
    You are adding error handling to a flow and choosing the *shape*. Observable input: is this the whole flow, one risky block, or a transient backend fault — and is the failing operation safe to repeat?
  • treeLogic placement — business rule vs Power Fx vs flow vs plug-in
    you have a validation/defaulting/derivation requirement and must decide **where the logic lives**. Observable trigger: "should this be a business rule, a flow, or a plug-in?" The tie-breaker is **enforcement reach** — must it hold for the Web API/imports/every app, or only in one UI?
  • treePA flow recovery — stuck / broken / off
    one or more Power Automate flows in a Dataverse-backed solution are off, throwing 404 on `For_a_selected_row_V2`, or otherwise misbehaving after a solution import. **Traverse this tree top-to-bottom before selecting a method — do NOT pattern-match on keywords in the user's situation description.**
  • treePCF — Which React surface?
    you have a UI requirement on the Power Platform that you're considering building as React, and you need to pick the *surface* before writing any code — a field/dataset on a form, a full standalone app, a simple themed input, a public-facing page, or custom React embedded in a model-driven app. **Traverse this tree top-to-bottom before selecting a surface — do NOT pattern-match on keywords in the user's situation description.**
  • treePlug-ins — pipeline stage + execution mode
    you've already decided the logic must be a Dataverse plug-in (not a business rule/flow — see the logic-placement tree below) and must choose the registration **stage** (PreValidation 10 / PreOperation 20 / PostOperation 40) and **mode** (sync / async). Observable trigger: you're filling in the Plug-in Registration Tool / `pac plugin` step and don't know which stage to pick.
  • treePower BI — Measure vs calculated column
    You are adding a derived value to a semantic model and must decide whether it is a measure, a calculated column, or a Power Query / source transform. Observable trigger: "I need a new field for X" where X is a number, a ratio, a classification, or a key.
  • treePower BI — Storage mode (Import / DirectQuery / Direct Lake / Composite)
    You are creating a new semantic model, or an existing model is slow, over-budget on capacity, or showing stale data, and you must choose (or change) how tables are stored. Observable triggers: report falls off a performance cliff at scale, refresh takes too long / can't fit, or stakeholders ask for "live" data.
  • treePower Fx state — named formula vs Set/OnStart vs With vs Concurrent
    you're deciding *where* a value lives in a canvas app — a derived value, a value to mutate, a one-shot scoped intermediate, or a set of independent data loads. Observable triggers: a monolithic `App.OnStart` delaying first paint, `Set()` scattered across control events, a delegation-breaking aggregate inside a row predicate, or sequential `ClearCollect` chains.
  • treePower Pages vs a custom React/Fluent site
  • treePower Pages — Authentication provider choice
    Standing up auth on a new external-facing Power Pages site, or deciding whether to migrate an existing one. Observable trigger: "which login should customers use" / "should we move off B2C".
  • treePower Pages — Granting a portal user access to a row
    A portal user (anonymous or authenticated) needs to see/create/edit a Dataverse row in Power Pages, or a row is unexpectedly visible/invisible in Pages while behaving differently in the model-driven app. Observable triggers: "the list is blank", "they can submit but not see their submission", "this row shows in MDA but not Pages".
  • treeProgrammatic flow creation — Approach A vs B
    You must create / update / delete cloud flows *programmatically* (a script, not the designer) — typically bulk. Observable inputs: does a service-principal token come back from the PA Management API with a usable `roles` claim, and does the SPN hold Dataverse `System Administrator` (or create/update on the `workflow` table)?
  • treeReuse — child flow vs inline vs other surface
    You have a sequence of actions and must decide whether to inline it, extract it into a child flow, or push it to another platform tier. Observable input: how many call sites reuse it, how big the parent already is, and whether the logic belongs lower on the mechanism ladder.
  • treeSecurity — record access mechanism (role scope vs team vs sharing)
    "user/group X needs to see/edit records Y" and you must pick the access mechanism. Observable trigger: a security requirement phrased as who-sees-what. Decisive axes: **does the boundary follow the org chart**, is it a **stable group** or **ad-hoc**, and is it **whole-row** or **one column**.
  • treeSolution mechanics — managed or unmanaged for THIS environment?
    You are about to export or import a solution and must choose its managed state for a specific target environment — DEV, TEST/UAT, PROD, a throwaway sandbox, or a downstream env you're trying to "fix in place."
  • treeTriggers — which trigger type?
    You are starting a new cloud flow and must pick its trigger. The observable inputs are: *what kicks it off* (a data change, a person, a clock, an external system) and *where the data lives* (Dataverse, SharePoint, a mailbox, an external API).
  • treewhich environment tier?
Best practices 58
  • Primary diagnostic — the first wrong turn when a Dataverse read fails.A Dataverse 401/403/404 is an access error, never proof a field is missing
  • Absolute rule — a *When a row is modified* flow that writes back to the same row with no recursion guard is an infinite loop waiting for the first edit. It will burn your daily request budget and can throttle the whole environment.A Dataverse-triggered flow that updates its own trigger row must break the recursion loop
  • Absolute rule — exporting from dev and importing to prod with nothing in between is not a pipeline, it's a loaded gun.A pipeline has a test stage between dev and prod — always
  • Pattern — strong default; manual assignment is allowed only for a tiny fixed admin set with a written reason.Assign web roles via Dataverse automation, not by hand
  • Absolute rule — ignoring service-protection limits gets your principal throttled or blocked.Batch bulk Dataverse writes and honor service-protection limits — back off on 429, don't hammer
  • Pattern — strong default for new PCF work; non-virtual is legacy.Build new PCF controls as React virtual controls and declare platform libraries — don't bundle your own React/Fluent
  • Absolute rule — a role model where everyone is an admin is the absence of a security model.Build security roles by copying Basic User and granting least privilege — never System Administrator "so they can work"
  • Pattern — strong default; a code app is the heaviest app surface, gated by an explicit not-supported list.Choose a Power Apps code app only when you need full DOM/framework control — and check its unsupported-feature list first
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason recorded on the column.Choose choice vs lookup vs customer column by who owns the value list and whether you need polymorphism
  • Primary diagnostic — when an SPN gets HTTP 401 from `api.flow.microsoft.com`, switch surfaces; don't retry auth.Create cloud flows programmatically through the Dataverse Web API, not the PA Management API
  • Primary diagnostic — when a Copilot Studio agent hallucinates, check the knowledge sources first, not the model.Curate grounding sources over collecting them, and enable strict grounding
  • Pattern — strong default; build a PCF only with a written reason the cheaper surfaces were ruled out.Default to "no PCF" — reach for a custom control only when a cheaper surface genuinely can't deliver the UI
  • Pattern — the default for cross-environment data movement and natural-key lookups.Define alternate keys on natural keys and use Upsert to import GUID-free across environments
  • Absolute rule — shipping an RLS model you only tested as the author is a security defect.Define RLS on dimensions, and test it "View as role" with a real account
  • Absolute rule — a public-facing Power Pages site with a leaky Anonymous Read permission is a data-breach headline, not a bug.Design table permissions before forms, and verify the Anonymous role can't Read
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason (a genuinely API-less system). Choosing RPA where a REST call exists is a named anti-pattern. (House rule §4 + `flow-engineer` opinion "desktop flows are last-resort.")Desktop flows (RPA) are the last-resort tier — if the system has an API, build a connector instead
  • Pattern — strong default for any tenant handling real corporate data. Deviate only where a documented business case and active monitoring justify opening a connector.DLP is default-deny: classify Business deliberately, block by exception
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason recorded on the relationship.Don't put Cascade-All (delete/share/assign) on a relationship to a high-volume child table
  • Pattern — the platform-wide §3 #7 rule applied to "where does this logic live."Enforce business logic at the lowest tier that holds: business rule → Power Fx → flow → plug-in
  • Absolute rule — a SharePoint URL, list ID, or feature flag baked into a solution component is config that should have travelled as data, and it will be wrong in every environment except the one it was authored in.Environment variables for config that varies per environment, never hard-coded
  • Absolute rule — a production cloud flow with no error scope and no retry decision is a bug, not a style preference. (House rule §3 #10.)Every production flow gets a top-level Try-Catch-Finally and a deliberate retry policy
  • Absolute rule — a solution-aware flow that ships an embedded connection or a hard-coded site URL / list ID / env GUID is a broken import in the next environment. (House rule §3 #2 and #3.)Every solution-aware flow uses connection references and environment variables — never embedded connections or hard-coded values
  • Pattern — strong default; a parent flow that inlines the same 5-action sequence three times is a refactor target, not a finished build. (House rule §3 "lowest-tier mechanism" + `flow-engineer` opinion "child flows for any logic used 2+ times.")Extract logic used 2+ times into a child flow with explicit input/output schemas
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason (e.g. you genuinely need a run record for every event for audit).Filter at the trigger, not at runtime — use trigger conditions, not a Condition action
  • Absolute rule — a test that mutates shared or production data and leaves it behind is a defect in the test.Isolate test data and tear it down — never test against shared/prod state
  • Pattern — strong default; an agent with no escalation path is a liability, not a product.Make escalation-to-human a designed feature, with context handoff
  • Absolute rule — violations build an invisible unmanaged layer that *will* cause "why didn't my fix flow through" later.Managed in test and prod, unmanaged only in dev
  • Absolute rule — a flat-table Power BI model is a defect, not a style choice.Model a star schema, not one flat wide table
  • Pattern — strong default. A flow built from deeply nested one-line expressions with no `Compose` steps is unreviewable and fails intermittently on null paths.Name intermediate values with Compose and make every cross-action reference null-safe
  • Absolute rule — string-built FetchXML from `request` parameters is an injection vector on a public site.Never concatenate request input into FetchXML/Liquid, and treat hiding as cosmetic
  • Absolute rule — if TEST and PROD run different `.zip` bytes, TEST proved nothing about PROD. Re-packing per stage reintroduces exactly the variance the pipeline exists to remove.One managed artifact, built once, promoted unchanged to every stage
  • Pattern — strong default; climb the tiers only when the lower one can't express the requirement.Pick the lowest-tier mechanism for a derived value: formula/calculated → rollup → plug-in → flow
  • Primary diagnostic — when a model is slow, over-budget on capacity, or stale, check storage mode first.Pick the storage mode deliberately — Import by default, DirectQuery/Direct Lake/Composite with a reason
  • Pattern — strong default. Mismatched or default (`cr_`, `new_`) prefixes across solutions in one repo are an ALM nightmare to merge and make customizations untraceable.Pin one publisher prefix you control, per repo
  • Pattern — strong default; reserve `App.OnStart` `Set` for non-derivable side effects.Prefer named formulas and `With()` over `Set()` in `OnStart` for derived state
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason.Put business logic in measures, not calculated columns
  • Pattern — strong default; build canvas for the same job only with a written reason.Put heavy, structured, record-centric data entry on a model-driven form — not a hand-built canvas screen
  • Pattern — a deliberate, narrow tool; broad use is a performance and complexity tax.Reach for column-level (field) security only to hide an individual sensitive column — not as a general access model
  • Pattern — strong default. Promote a workload up the environment tiers as users, data sensitivity, business impact, or ALM need increases; never run real production in the Default environment.Real workloads get dedicated environments, sized by users + sensitivity + ALM
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason on the registration step.Register a Dataverse plug-in step on the stage + mode the work actually needs
  • Pattern — strong default; the boundary test below decides each intent.Route high-stakes intents to authored topics, everything else to generative answers
  • Absolute rule — a secret typed into a solution component is committed to git, shipped in the managed `.zip`, and visible to anyone who can export the solution. That is credential disclosure, not configuration.Secrets live in Key Vault, referenced — never as plaintext env-var defaults
  • Absolute rule — the Default environment is every user's open sandbox by default, and unrestricted environment creation is how shadow IT proliferates. Both are the #1 governance gaps in an ungoverned tenant.Secure the Default environment and restrict who can create environments
  • Pattern — strong default. A loop running at the silent default of 20, with pagination off, over a source that returns more than its default page, is three latent bugs in one action.Set Apply-to-each concurrency deliberately, turn on pagination, and prefer batch over per-row loops
  • Absolute rule — a flow shipped with a baked-in connection ID is the #1 cause of solution import failures and silently-wrong-credential bugs.Solution-aware flows use connection references, never hard-coded connections
  • Absolute rule — a `.zip` in git is opaque, un-reviewable, and merge-hostile. Violations defeat the entire point of putting a solution under source control.Source-control the unpacked solution tree, never the .zip
  • Absolute rule for this plugin — every Power Platform recommendation that touches a premium connector, AI Builder, a license tier, or Dataverse capacity states the cost impact. A design that ignores licensing is a design that gets vetoed at procurement.State the licensing and capacity impact of every recommendation
  • Pattern — strong default for any non-trivial measure; the cheapest correctness evidence Power BI offers.Test DAX measures as code — EVALUATE against a frozen dataset, expected vs actual
  • Absolute rule — export success is not import success. A release you've only exported is a release you've never deployed.Test the import on a fresh environment before declaring a release done
  • Absolute rule — validating access only as a system admin / author is a non-test that ships security defects.Test under the real user's security context — "worked as admin" is not a test
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a Monitor trace that justifies it.Treat every canvas property as a function the runtime re-evaluates — budget network calls and control count
  • Pattern — strong default; exceed the budget only with a measured justification.Treat model-driven form `OnLoad` as a performance budget — async with returned Promises, ≤3 web API calls, batched
  • Absolute rule — a non-delegable query against a source that can exceed the row cap is a bug, not a preference.Treat the delegation warning as a P1 design constraint, not a polish item
  • Absolute rule — a PCF that leaks listeners/timers in `destroy()` or fires `notifyOutputChanged()` from inside `updateView()` is a bug.Treat the PCF lifecycle as a contract — clean up in `destroy()` and keep `updateView` idempotent
  • Pattern — strong default for any production environment, given the premium licensing it requires. The sharing limit and weekly digest alone justify the SKU for prod.Turn on Managed Environments for production; set sharing limits
  • Pattern — strong default. Reaching for patches as the routine release vehicle stacks solution layers in PROD and leaves deleted components behind.Upgrade by default; patch only for hotfixes
  • Pattern — strong default; write JS only when a business rule genuinely can't express the logic.Use a business rule before a JavaScript web resource for simple show/hide/require/lock logic
  • Absolute rule — a canvas write with no error handling and no `Defaults()` is a bug.Write Dataverse records with `Patch(Defaults(table), ...)` wrapped in `IfError` — never a hand-rolled record literal
ravenclaude-core1 trees · 3 practices
Decision trees 1
  • treeTeam Lead — which specialist to spawn
    a user request has just arrived at the Team Lead session, and the Team Lead is choosing whether to handle it directly or delegate to one specialist (or a sequence). **Traverse top-to-bottom before spawning — do NOT keyword-match the request to an agent name.**
Best practices 3
  • Absolute ruleHonor the three epistemic protocols as a triad
  • PatternTraverse the routing tree before spawning a specialist
  • PatternTurn on command review only where native auto-mode can't reach
regulatory-compliance6 trees · 22 practices
Decision trees 6
  • treeCDD vs EDD vs SDD by customer risk
    a customer is being onboarded or periodically refreshed, and the next decision is *how deep the due diligence goes*. Observable trigger: a new relationship, a periodic-review date, or an existing customer crossing into a higher-risk product. Do NOT use this to decide *whether* to report activity (that is the reportability tree) — this is about due-diligence depth only.
  • treeControl type — preventive vs detective vs corrective
    a control is being designed, mapped, or tested and the next decision is *what type it is* — because the type dictates the evidence that proves it works and how its failure shows up. Observable trigger: building a control matrix, mapping a control to an obligation, scoping a control test. Rating "effective" without classifying type first is the failure this prevents.
  • treeIs this reportable — SAR/STR, threshold report, or none
    a transaction, alert, or pattern has surfaced and the next decision is *what reporting obligation (if any) fires*. Observable trigger: a monitoring alert, a referral, an unusual instruction. Decide the **category** before drafting anything — reaching for the wrong instrument is the failure this prevents.
  • treePolicy vs procedure vs standard vs guideline
    something needs to be written down and the next decision is *which document layer it belongs in*. Observable trigger: a new regulation to implement, a process change, a gap analysis output, a request to "document this." Picking the wrong layer is what guarantees drift — procedure buried in policy, or a mandatory rule written as a guideline.
  • treeRegulator finding — severity triage
    a regulator has issued a written finding, communication, or enforcement-related document to the firm (or a subsidiary), and the next decision is _what severity tier this is_ and _which response playbook fires_. Do NOT use this tree for internal audit findings, second-line control-test findings, or self-identified issues raised by the firm itself — those use separate workflows.
  • treeWhich regulator / regime applies (scope before you map)
    before mapping a control, classifying a finding, or using a threshold word ("material", "significant", "reportable"), the next decision is *which regulator and regime govern* — because the same word and the same control answer differently across regimes. Observable trigger: any cross-border entity, a Bermuda-domiciled entity, or a term/threshold about to be applied. Carrying a definition or timeline across a jurisdiction boundary is the silent failure this prevents.
Best practices 22
  • Absolute ruleA SAR/STR narrative answers *why* it looks suspicious — name the typology, not the transaction
  • Primary diagnosticClassify a regulator finding's severity before you pick a response playbook
  • Primary diagnosticClassify the control type before you rate it — preventive, detective, and corrective fail differently
  • Primary diagnosticDecide reportability before you draft — suspicion-based, threshold-based, or no report
  • Absolute ruleDocument the no-file decision — a "we decided not to report" is itself a record
  • Absolute ruleEDD adds verification depth, senior approval, and a recorded rationale — not just more documents
  • Absolute ruleEvery PBC item ships with its evidence pack — "complete" with nothing attached is not complete
  • Absolute ruleExplain every material variance vs prior period before you submit
  • Absolute ruleFix the source, not the return — never apply a hidden adjustment inside a filing
  • PatternFrame conduct and consumer-outcome obligations as outcomes, not box-ticking clauses
  • Absolute ruleInherent, residual, and target are three separate ratings — a two-rating register hides the truth
  • Absolute ruleKeep policy and procedure in separate documents — mix them and drift is guaranteed
  • Absolute ruleMaker-checker is two people — a return signed off by one person has no second set of eyes
  • Absolute ruleMap one control to one requirement — keep the obligation→control→evidence chain traceable
  • Absolute ruleNo control statement ships without a regulatory cite and evidence it operates
  • Absolute ruleRemediation has a named owner, a date, and an independent tester — never the person who built the broken control
  • Absolute ruleRetain records on a written schedule keyed to the regime — not on disposal instinct
  • Absolute ruleRisk-rate the customer before you choose CDD depth — SDD, CDD, or EDD
  • Absolute ruleSanctions screening hygiene — capture the list version, re-screen the deltas, and dispose every hit
  • Absolute ruleScope the jurisdiction and regime before you map a control or a term
  • Absolute ruleSource-trace every load-bearing cell — a return value you can't reproduce will not survive an exam
  • Absolute ruleTune transaction-monitoring thresholds with data, never with opinion
salesforce31 trees · 49 practices
Decision trees 31
  • treeagent or deterministic automation?
  • treeApex Security — where to enforce CRUD/FLS (with sharing / WITH SECURITY_ENFORCED / stripInaccessible / USER_MODE)
    You are writing Apex that reads or writes records on behalf of a user (a controller, a service called from LWC/Aura/VF, a user-invoked action). Observable triggers: a PMD/CRUD-FLS reviewer finding, an `@AuraEnabled` method exposing data, or a `without sharing` keyword with no justification. The *verdict* escalates to `ravenclaude-core/security-reviewer`; this tree picks the *mechanism*.
  • treeAsync Apex — which channel (Future / Queueable / Batch / Scheduled / Platform Event)
    You have work that must leave the synchronous transaction — a callout from a trigger context, a roll-up over more rows than a synchronous query can hold, a job that must chain, or a recurring task. Observable triggers: `System.LimitException` from synchronous volume, "callout not allowed from trigger," a need to run on a schedule, or an `@future` that won't compile because you passed an sObject.
  • treeAutomation — Agentforce agent vs Flow/Apex?
    A task could plausibly be "an AI agent", and you must decide whether it should be. Observable trigger: "can Agentforce do this?", "should this be an agent?", or a stakeholder asking for an AI assistant over a fixed-path process.
  • treeAutomation — which Flow type?
  • treeBuild method — declarative vs programmatic for this requirement?
    A new automation or customization requirement, and you must pick the build tool. Observable trigger: "should this be a Flow or Apex?", a new field-update/validation/automation request, or a record-trigger being scoped.
  • treeBulk Safety — is this Apex safe for a 200-record load?
    You are reviewing or writing any Apex that runs in a trigger, a Batch `execute`, or any path a data load can reach. Observable triggers: `Too many SOQL queries: 101`, `Too many DML statements: 151`, `Apex CPU time limit exceeded`, or a test that only inserts one record.
  • treeData — how to open record access (OWD / hierarchy / sharing rules / manual / Apex-managed)
    You're designing who can see a record type and choosing among OWD, role hierarchy, sharing rules, manual shares, and Apex managed sharing. Observable trigger: "user/group X can't see record Y" or a new object's sharing design.
  • treeData — is this query LDV-safe?
    You're authoring or debugging a SOQL query against a high-volume object (millions of rows), or you hit `Non-selective query against large object type`, a query timeout, or a slow list view/report.
  • treeData — which load tool (Data Loader vs Bulk API vs ETL)
    You must insert/update/upsert/delete a dataset into an org and are choosing the tool. Observable triggers: a row count beyond a handful, a recurring sync, or a multi-source transform.
  • treeDeployment — change set vs Metadata API vs SFDX/CI?
    Metadata is ready to move between orgs and you must choose the *mechanism*. Observable trigger: "how do we get this to UAT/prod?" or someone reaches for the Setup → Outbound Change Sets UI.
  • treeFlow or Apex?
  • treehow to package and ship
  • treeIntegration & Data — at-least-once event delivery, is the consumer safe?
    You subscribe to Platform Events or CDC and must confirm the consumer won't double-process a redelivered event or silently miss events. Observable triggers: duplicate downstream records, a consumer that was offline, or designing any event subscriber.
  • treeIntegration — synchronous vs asynchronous callout placement
    Salesforce must call an external system and you're deciding whether the callout runs inline or async. Observable triggers: a callout in a trigger/save path, or the error `You have uncommitted work pending`, or a callout looping per record.
  • treeIntegration — which pattern (request-reply vs fire-forget vs batch vs pub-sub)
    You must move data or notifications between Salesforce and another system and have not yet chosen REST/SOAP vs Platform Events vs CDC vs Bulk API. Observable trigger: a requirement phrased as "when X happens here, do Y there" or "load/sync this dataset."
  • treeis this code bulk-safe?
  • treeis this query LDV-safe?
  • treeLifecycle — which environment do I build/test this in?
    Choosing where a given piece of work happens — a feature, a perf test, UAT sign-off, a hotfix. Observable trigger: "which org should I do this in?", or someone about to make a change directly in production or a shared sandbox.
  • treeLWC communication — @api props/events vs LMS vs public method?
  • treeLWC data access — LDS vs wire-Apex vs imperative-Apex?
  • treeopening up record access
  • treeOrg strategy — single org vs multi-org?
    Deciding how many *production* orgs the business runs, or whether a new business unit / acquisition / region needs its own org. Observable trigger: "do we need a second org?", an M&A integration, or a data-residency requirement landing.
  • treePackaging — which package type for this metadata?
    You have a coherent set of metadata (objects, code, config) and must decide *how it is packaged and distributed*. Observable trigger: you're about to run `sf package create`, or someone asks "should this be unlocked, managed, or just deployed?"
  • treePlacement — Flow vs Apex vs LWC for a requirement?
  • treeReactive-read freshness — wire vs refreshApex vs imperative re-fetch?
  • treeSOQL Selectivity — will this query stay index-backed at volume?
    You are authoring or fixing a SOQL query that will run against an object expected to grow large (orders, cases, line items, logs). Observable triggers: `System.QueryException: Non-selective query against large object type`, a slow query in production, or a Query Plan showing cost ≥ 1.0.
  • treestructuring trigger logic
  • treeTrigger Logic — where does this belong (before/after, handler, async, Flow)?
    You have logic to attach to a DML event and must decide the trigger context and placement. Observable triggers: a new automation requirement on an object, a field that must be defaulted/validated, a roll-up, or a callout that needs to happen on save.
  • treewhich async primitive?
  • treewhich integration pattern?
Best practices 49
  • Primary diagnostic — when this LimitException appears, the SOQL-in-a-loop is the first thing to check.`Too many SOQL queries: 101` means a query is inside a loop — find it first
  • Absolute rule for the controller's security posture; pattern for the cacheable/bulk shape.An LWC's Apex controller is cacheable for reads, FLS-aware, with sharing, and bulk-safe
  • Pattern — strong default for any object whose row count grows unbounded over time; deviate only when every row stays operationally hot.Archive cold rows to keep the hot object small — tier data before it becomes an LDV fire
  • Primary diagnostic — when DML on a high-volume object is slow or row-locks, check for ownership/lookup skew first.Avoid ownership and lookup skew — keep any one parent/owner under the skew threshold
  • Absolute rule — string concatenation of untrusted input into SOQL is an injection vulnerability; the verdict escalates to core.Bind every variable in dynamic SOQL — never concatenate user input
  • Absolute rule — a callout inside an open DML transaction is a runtime error; the cumulative limits are a design constraint, not a runtime surprise.Budget the callout limits and never call out from a trigger synchronously
  • Pattern — strong default; accessibility requirements are non-negotiable for any user-facing component.Build accessible, render-cheap LWCs — base components, keyed lists, and deferred work
  • Absolute rule — `SeeAllData=true` is banned; tests create their own data.Build test data with a TestDataFactory — never `@isTest(SeeAllData=true)`
  • Absolute rule — a violation is a production bug, not a style preference.Bulkify every SOQL and DML — no query or DML inside a loop
  • Pattern — strong default; an empty or over-broad `catch` is a defect, not a style choice.Catch deliberately, throw typed custom exceptions, never swallow
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason in the design.Choose Flow vs Apex deliberately — one automation entry point per object
  • Pattern — strong default; the choice must be justified against the workload, not picked by habit.Choose the async channel deliberately — Batch, Queueable, Future, or Scheduled
  • Pattern — strong default; escalate to a heavier channel only when the lighter one cannot reach.Communicate by the smallest mechanism that reaches — props down, events up, LMS only across the DOM
  • Pattern — strong default; a multi-org split is a deliberate, expensive, written decision.Default to a single org with a tiered environment chain — go multi-org only for a hard reason
  • Absolute rule — security defaults are not preferences; the verdict escalates to core.Default to least access — `with sharing` and enforce CRUD/FLS in user-context code
  • Pattern — strong default for any large ownership/role/sharing-affecting load; deviate only when the load is small or sharing-neutral.Defer sharing recalculation during large loads — don't pay the recalc cost per row
  • Pattern — strong default; manual prod deletions are a pipeline bug.Delete metadata through a tracked destructiveChanges manifest, applied after the additive deploy — never by clicking in the org
  • Pattern — strong default for package/feature development; deviate only with a written reason.Develop in scratch orgs with source tracking — sandboxes are not the source of truth
  • Pattern — strong default once the same logic appears in a second Flow.Extract repeated Flow logic into autolaunched subflows
  • Absolute rule on high-volume objects — a non-selective query on an LDV object is a runtime error (`Non-selective query against large object type`), not a slow query.Filter SOQL on indexed, selective fields — defeat the non-selective-query error before it ships
  • Absolute rule — an ungrounded answer or an unconfirmed irreversible action is a defect, not a UX choice.Ground every agent action in real data and guard every side effect — the model plans, deterministic actions execute
  • Absolute rule — a handler that issues DML on its own object with no guard is a defect.Guard every trigger handler against recursion — a static guard is mandatory
  • Absolute rule. A DML or query element inside a Flow Loop is a bug, not a preference.Keep Flows bulk-safe — no Get/Create/Update/Delete inside a Loop element
  • Absolute rule — never put an agent where a deterministic automation belongs, and never ship one ungated by the Trust Layer.Make Agentforce earn its non-determinism — deterministic work stays deterministic
  • Absolute rule — a non-idempotent consumer that double-processes a redelivered event is a data-corruption bug, not a tuning issue.Make event consumers idempotent and replay-aware — delivery is at-least-once, not exactly-once
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason naming who owns the event shape and who consumes it.Match the notification mechanism to who owns the schema — Platform Events vs Change Data Capture vs callout
  • Pattern — strong default for internal modular development; deviate only with a written reason.Modularize the org into unlocked 2GP packages with declared dependencies — not one giant force-app
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason naming the row count and why synchronous calls win at that volume.Move bulk datasets with Bulk API 2.0 — not row-by-row synchronous REST/SOAP
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written, ordered reason in the design.One record-triggered Flow per object per save phase — and order it
  • Absolute rule — a second trigger on the same object, or logic in a trigger body, is a defect.One trigger per object — the trigger dispatches, the handler thinks
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason and a migration note.Package metadata in 2GP and deploy in dependency order — never click-deploy to prod
  • Pattern — strong default; the prod test level is effectively fixed by the platform.Pick the deploy test level deliberately — RunLocalTests for prod, NoTestRun only in scratch/dev
  • Pattern — strong default; the type follows the trigger, not personal familiarity.Pick the Flow type by what triggers it and when it must run
  • Pattern — strong default; deviate only with a written reason naming the row count, cadence, and source-system complexity.Pick the load tool by volume and repeatability — Data Loader vs Bulk API vs ETL
  • Pattern — strong default for read paths; use imperative Apex only for user-triggered actions or writes.Prefer @wire to a cacheable method over imperative Apex for reads
  • Absolute rule on high-volume objects — a non-selective query is a `QueryException` waiting for the row count to grow.Query selectively — filter on indexed fields so the optimizer can use them
  • Pattern — strong default for any new automation or customization; deviate only with a written reason.Reach for declarative before code — write Apex only past the declarative ceiling, and document the call
  • Pattern — strong default; write Apex only when LDS cannot express the need.Reach for Lightning Data Service / UI API before writing any Apex
  • Pattern — a tuning lever for proven hot paths, not a day-one default; deviate only after the selective-query and data-model levers are exhausted.Reach for skinny tables and custom indexes when hot queries strain at LDV — but treat them as Support-managed levers
  • Pattern — strong default; a sprawling, vaguely-instructed topic is a reliability and safety liability.Scope each topic to one bounded job with explicit instructions — many narrow topics beat one do-everything agent
  • Pattern — strong default; widening the org-wide default is a one-way door at scale, so start closed and justify every loosening.Set OWD to the most restrictive default that still works — then open access deliberately
  • Absolute rule for fault handling; strong-default pattern for entry conditions.Set tight entry conditions and a fault path on every record-triggered Flow
  • Absolute rule for environment-varying configuration — a hard-coded ID or a config-bearing data record is a bug.Store configuration as deployable metadata (Custom Metadata Types), not as data records or hard-coded values
  • Absolute rule at volume — a single SOQL that exceeds the per-transaction row limit throws; a heap-busting in-memory list is a `LimitException`, not a slow path.Stream large result sets with Batch Apex QueryLocator — not one 50k-row SOQL
  • Pattern — strong default; shipping a non-deterministic agent on a single happy-path demo is negligence.Test an agent with an evaluation set, not a demo — and keep measuring it after it ships
  • Pattern — strong default; a nested loop where a map keyed lookup would do is a CPU-time and readability defect.Use `Map` for O(1) lookups — never a nested loop to correlate records
  • Pattern — strong default; use after-save (or Apex) only when the work is not a same-record write.Use a before-save record-triggered Flow for same-record field updates
  • Absolute rule — a hard-coded endpoint or secret in Apex is a security and portability bug, and the secret-handling verdict escalates to core.Use Named Credentials for callout endpoints and secrets — never hard-code them
  • Absolute rule — a prod deploy that wasn't validated check-only first is a bug in the pipeline.Validate check-only on every PR — the real deploy must never be the first time tests run against prod
tableau14 trees · 26 practices
Decision trees 14
  • treeCalculations — Basic aggregate vs LOD vs table calc
    A number must be computed and you must choose the calc *layer*. Observable trigger: a "wrong total / double-counting" bug, or a new measure whose required grain you can state relative to the view. This is the most consequential calc decision and the source of most wrong-number bugs.
  • treeConnection mode — extract vs live
    A data source is modeled and you must choose extract (Hyper) or live. Observable triggers: "extract or live?"; a dashboard slow on every interaction against a transactional DB; a stakeholder asking "how fresh is this?"
  • treeConnection model — relationship vs join vs blend
    You have two or more tables/datasets to combine in a single data source, and you must choose *how* they connect. Observable triggers: "should this be a relationship, a join, or a blend?"; a `SUM` that double-counts after combining tables; a number that won't filter by a field from the second dataset.
  • treeContent promotion — CMT vs REST API vs tabcmd vs manual
    Content must move between environments — dev→test→prod — and you're choosing *how*. Observable trigger: "promote this dashboard to prod," "set up our release pipeline," or a connection-string/permission drift discovered after a hand-republish.
  • treeDiagnosing a slow workbook (the Performance Recorder ladder)
    A workbook is slow and you need the *cause*, not a guess. Observable triggers: "this dashboard takes 40 seconds to load"; a sheet that hangs on filter change. Always turn on the **Performance Recorder**, reproduce the slow action, and read the **longest events first** before touching anything.
  • treeEmbedding auth — Connected Apps/JWT vs SAML SSO vs public
    A viz is being embedded in an external/portal/customer-facing app and you're choosing how the embedded session authenticates. Observable trigger: "embed this dashboard in our portal," "embed with per-tenant isolation," or a legacy embed still using trusted tickets. This is a **security control**: the chosen leaf escalates to `ravenclaude-core/security-reviewer`.
  • treeInteractivity — Which mechanism (filter / highlight / parameter / set action)
    The user wants the dashboard to respond to interaction ("make these views talk", "let me drill", "let the user choose"). Observable trigger: a stated interaction goal you can phrase as "when the user does X, the dashboard should Y."
  • treeLOD — FIXED vs INCLUDE vs EXCLUDE
    You have already decided the answer needs an **LOD** (from the tree above) and must pick the keyword. Observable trigger: you can name the grain the number must compute at and whether it is absolute or relative to the current view.
  • treeNext-gen surface — Tableau Pulse vs Tableau Next vs classic dashboard
    A stakeholder wants to "track a metric" and you're choosing the surface. Observable trigger: "should this be Pulse or a dashboard?", "automate this KPI with insights," or a request to build agentic/semantic-layer analytics on Data Cloud. **This whole tree is volatile** — the next-gen surface moves every quarter; mark every leaf `[verify-at-build]` before quoting.
  • treePermission model — locked project vs per-content grants
    You are setting up (or auditing) who can see and do what with content on a Tableau Server/Cloud site. The observable trigger: a request like "Finance and Sales must not see each other's workbooks," a request to grant a new group access, or an audit finding that the same user has different effective permissions on two workbooks in the same project. Not for *row*-level visibility (that is the RLS tree).
  • treeRLS mechanism — user filter vs entitlements + data policy vs separate content
    Different viewers of the *same* content must see *different rows* — "each regional manager sees only their region," "each customer sees only their own tenant's data." The observable trigger is a per-viewer row-visibility requirement on shared content. This is a **security control**: every leaf here ends in escalation to `ravenclaude-core/security-reviewer`.
  • treeViz design — Chart type by question class
    The user asks "which chart should I use?" or hands you a measure/dimension set with a business question, and you must pick a mark/encoding. The observable trigger is a stated business question whose *class* (comparison, trend, distribution, correlation, part-to-whole, geographic) can be named.
  • treeWhere to do the work — Prep vs calculated fields vs reshape at source
    A transformation is needed (pivot, union, dedup, cleanup, derived column, aggregation) and you must decide *where* it lives. Observable triggers: the same calc copy-pasted across many workbooks; a row-level calc that recomputes on every query; a pivot/union that won't model cleanly in the data source.
  • treeWhere to filter — source vs data-source vs context vs dimension
    You're adding a filter (or a slow workbook over-fetches rows it then filters away in the view). Observable triggers: a region/date-scoped dashboard scanning the full table; a `FIXED` LOD or top-N that must operate on a filtered subset; a quick filter slow to populate.
Best practices 26
  • Pattern — blending is the connection method of last resort; a relationship or join is almost always the correct first choice.A data blend is a last resort — relate or join first, blend only across unrelatable sources
  • Absolute rule — interactive admin credentials in a promotion/automation script are a secret-leak and a single point of failure. Headless auth uses Connected Apps or Personal Access Tokens.Authenticate automation with Connected Apps / PATs, never an admin password in a script
  • Pattern — a Prep flow that is not idempotent is a data-quality incident waiting to happen; design for safe re-runs from the start.Build Prep flows to be idempotent and incremental — re-running yields the same output, not duplicates
  • Pattern — a certified, published data source is the strong default governed grain; an embedded per-workbook extract is the deviation you justify.Certify a governed published data source; build workbooks on it, not on private extracts
  • Pattern — performance is a design constraint applied while building (filter at source, minimize marks, keep the hot path cheap), not a rescue operation after a dashboard is already slow.Design dashboard performance in — don't tune it later
  • Absolute rule — a token that authenticates the right user against the wrong entitlement key still leaks. The combined design escalates to `ravenclaude-core/security-reviewer`.Design the JWT subject/scope and the RLS entitlement key together
  • Absolute rule — a truncated quantitative axis, an unsynchronized dual axis, or a two-point "trend" misleads the reader, and a misleading chart is a defect regardless of how it looks.Don't let the axis lie — zero-baseline bars, synchronized dual axes, real trends
  • Absolute rule — trusted tickets and embedded service-account credentials are deprecated/insecure. The embedding-auth *verdict* escalates to `ravenclaude-core/security-reviewer`.Embed with Connected Apps + a signed JWT, not trusted tickets or embedded credentials
  • Pattern — a Hyper extract is the strong default; a live connection is the deviation you justify with a written freshness need.Extract by default; choose live only against a named freshness requirement
  • Pattern — push every filter as close to the source as the requirement allows; a workbook-level dimension filter is the *last* place to remove rows, not the first.Filter at the cheapest layer — the fastest query is the row that never enters the workbook
  • Pattern — format every shipped view for fast reading and for users who can't perceive color, contrast, or fine detail; treat accessibility as a build requirement, not a polish step.Format for legibility and accessibility — color is never the only channel
  • Pattern — the locked-project + group-grant model is the strong default for any governed site; a per-content override is the deviation you justify and time-box.Grant permissions to groups on locked projects, not workbook-by-workbook
  • Absolute rule — row-level security is an access control; the *verdict* escalates to `ravenclaude-core/security-reviewer`. A user filter is a convenience, not a control.Implement RLS as an enforced data policy, not a hidden workbook filter
  • Absolute rule — mixing a row-level expression with an aggregate one in the same calc is a build error, and aggregating the wrong layer is a silent wrong-number bug.Know whether a calc is row-level or aggregate before you write it
  • Absolute rule — chart type is determined by the *class of question* (comparison / trend / distribution / correlation / part-to-whole / geographic), not by what looks impressive.Let the question pick the chart, never the aesthetic
  • Primary diagnostic — when a view is slow *after* the query returns, suspect mark count and high-cardinality quick filters first.Minimize marks and the things that issue queries — rendering scales with marks, latency with quick filters
  • Pattern — relationships (the logical layer) are the strong default for any multi-table model; a physical join is the deviation you justify in writing.Model with relationships first; drop to a physical join only with a reason
  • Pattern — match the interaction (filter action / highlight action / parameter / set action) to what the user is trying to *do*; reach past the default quick-filter only with a reason, but reach for the right mechanism deliberately.Pick the interactivity mechanism by intent, not by habit
  • Absolute rule — hand-republishing into a governed prod environment breaks the audit chain and drifts connections. Content moves through a repeatable migration path.Promote content dev→test→prod through a repeatable path; never hand-republish
  • Pattern — separated published data sources are the strong default; an embedded per-workbook extract is the deviation you justify.Publish data sources separately from workbooks; don't embed an extract per workbook
  • Pattern — when a number must be computed at a level of detail other than the viz, use an LOD expression; deviate only with a written reason.Reach for an LOD when the answer's grain differs from the viz's grain
  • Absolute rule — a table calculation left on default "Table (across)" addressing is a latent wrong-number bug that surfaces the day someone reorders pills or adds a dimension.Set table-calc addressing and partitioning explicitly — never ship the default
  • Pattern — an extract should carry only the columns, rows, and grain the viz needs; a full-fat mirror of the source is the deviation.Shape the extract to the question — hide, aggregate, materialize, then refresh incrementally
  • Pattern — promote a filter to context only when something downstream must see the reduced set; design dashboard sheets so the engine can *fuse* their queries rather than fragment them.Use context filters for one expensive early cut; let query fusion combine like-grained sheets
  • Pattern — sites are the tenancy boundary, projects are the governance unit; reaching for a new site when a project would do (or vice versa) is the deviation to avoid.Use sites for hard tenancy boundaries and projects for content governance — don't conflate them
  • Pattern — match the next-gen surface to the deliverable; deviate with a reason. **Volatile** — the Pulse / Tableau-Next / CRM-Analytics surface moves every quarter; `[verify-at-build]` every positioning claim.Use Tableau Pulse for a tracked metric; reserve a hand-built dashboard for rich exploration
web-design7 trees · 19 practices
Decision trees 7
  • treeAccessibility — native element vs ARIA widget
    You're building an interactive control (button, menu, modal, tabs, combobox, disclosure) and must decide whether to use a native HTML element, a native platform primitive (`<dialog>`/Popover), an accessible library primitive, or hand-rolled ARIA. The observable trigger: you're about to add `role=` / `tabindex` / `aria-*` to a `<div>` or `<span>`.
  • treeArchitecture — rendering strategy (SSG / SSG+ISR / SSR / RSC / islands / CSR)
    Choosing how a page (or route) is rendered for a new build or a re-platform. The observable trigger: you're picking a framework or a per-route render mode and need to justify deviating from static-first (house opinion #9: SSG > SSR > CSR).
  • treeCWV root-cause triage
    any CWV regression that needs diagnosis before fix-selection. **Traverse this tree top-to-bottom before picking a fix** — diagnosing the wrong vital wastes the budget.
  • treeDesign systems — reach for a token system / component foundation vs ad-hoc
    Deciding whether a styling decision should go through a token/design-system layer (or adopt a component foundation) or be a one-off. The observable trigger: you're about to type a hex, a magic spacing number, or `npm install` a component library — or the same value is appearing in a third place.
  • treePerformance — image format & loading choice
    Adding any image to a page and choosing its format and loading behavior. The observable trigger: you're writing an `<img>`/`<picture>` and must pick format, responsive sources, and `loading`/priority.
  • treePerformance — which Core Web Vital is failing → which fix
    Field data (CrUX / RUM at p75) shows a failing metric, or a page "feels" slow/janky/jumpy. The observable trigger is a specific failing metric, not a vibe — get the field number first. Thresholds: LCP < 2.5 s, INP < 200 ms, CLS < 0.1 `[verify-at-build — 2026 CWV thresholds]`.
  • treewhich component foundation?
Best practices 19
  • Pattern — static-first, HTML-first. The core content and primary actions work without client JS; interactivity is a layer on top, not a prerequisite (house opinions #9, #5).Build on a working HTML baseline, then enhance — don't require JS to render
  • Pattern — design narrowest first and scale up (house opinion #3); use fluid `clamp()` scales for type and space instead of stacking breakpoint overrides.Build responsive type and space mobile-first, fluid where it earns its keep
  • Absolute rule — every page declares its weight + LCP / CLS / INP targets before any code is written.Declare a Core Web Vitals budget before development starts
  • Pattern — every form is a conversion surface. Each field is friction and needs a reason; validation and errors are designed, not defaulted.Design forms for completion: label every field, validate on blur, name the fix
  • Absolute rule — keyboard operability is a P1 surface, equal to mouse/touch. A control a keyboard user cannot reach, activate, or escape is a WCAG 2.1.1 failure, not a polish gap.Every interactive surface must be fully keyboard-operable
  • Absolute rule — every animation has a no-motion fallback, and the UI survives forced-colors mode. Motion without a `prefers-reduced-motion` fallback is an anti-pattern (house opinion #13).Honor reduced-motion, forced-colors, and reduced-data preferences
  • Absolute rule — `:focus-visible` is shaped to the design system but never removed, and interactive targets meet the WCAG 2.2 minimum size.Keep focus visible and pointer targets large enough (WCAG 2.2 additions)
  • Primary diagnostic — when the page "feels laggy" on tap/type/click, INP is the metric, and long main-thread tasks are the first suspect.Keep INP under 200 ms by breaking up long tasks
  • Pattern — strong default. Web fonts are the #1 perf killer; treat every font as a budgeted asset with a deliberate loading strategy.Load web fonts with discipline — subset, preload critical, control the swap
  • Pattern — every shippable page has a title, meta description, canonical, OG/Twitter cards, a clean heading structure, and validating schema where it earns citations. SEO and a11y are one surface (house opinion #10).Make the page discoverable: semantic structure, complete metadata, valid schema
  • Pattern — at most one dominant call-to-action per screen (two max), and every screen ships its default, empty, loading, error, and success states.One CTA per screen, and design all five states of every screen
  • Absolute rule — the Largest Contentful Paint element is preloaded, priority-hinted, correctly sized, and never deferred. Lazy-loading the LCP image is a bug.Protect the LCP element with preload + priority, never lazy-load it
  • Absolute rule — semantic-HTML-first is a P1 design constraint, not a polish item.Reach for the semantic element before adding ARIA
  • Absolute rule — every element that arrives after first paint (image, font, ad, embed, injected banner) has its space reserved before it lands. CLS > 0.1 ships only with a written justification (house opinion #7).Reserve space for everything that loads late (no layout shift after first paint)
  • Absolute rule — color, type, spacing, radius, shadow, and motion all flow through named tokens. A hardcoded `#3b82f6` or `margin: 11px` in a component is a bug (house opinions #4, #12).Route every design decision through a token, never a hardcoded value
  • Pattern — strong default for every raster image: modern format, responsive `srcset`, dimensioned, and a loading strategy chosen by the image's position relative to the fold.Serve modern image formats responsively, and load them by position
  • Absolute rule — contrast ratios are checked at design time against the actual displayed background. A token that fails contrast is a bug, not a taste call.Treat color contrast as a constraint, not a preference
  • Pattern — CTAs are verbs with destination context, error messages name the fix, and terminology stays consistent across every surface.Write microcopy that acts: verb CTAs, fix-named errors, unified terms
  • Pattern — content leads with the answer, structures for scanning (headline → subhead → body → CTA), and holds one reading-level target per audience.Write to a scannable hierarchy at a held reading level

  

  



  

    
📜 Activity

    
    
  

  
Run history from .ravenclaude/runs/ — newest first. Each card is one multi-step run (its summary, structured-result status, and event count). Command-review verdicts live in the Review log tab.

Setting

    

    
What this controls

    

    
Examples

    
    
    
    Recommended level
    
    
    
      
    
        Working as an individual
        
      
    
      
    
        Working as a team
        
      
    
    
    
    
    When to relax or tighten
    
    
    
  
    

    


    
  
    
    
    
    Pattern
    
    
    What this is
    
    
    
    
    Why it's here
    
    
    
  
    

    


    
  
    
    
    
    How layers merge
    
    
    Claude Code reads three settings files and merges them at runtime. The strictest rule across all three always wins — a personal “allow” cannot loosen a team “ask” or “deny”.
    
    
    
      User
      ~/.claude/settings.json — applies to every project on your machine. Only you see it; it is never committed to git. Use it for personal preferences that apply everywhere.
    
    
    
    
      Local
      .claude/settings.local.json — project-specific but gitignored. Stays on your machine. Use it for project-level tweaks you do not want to share with teammates.
    
    
    
    
      Project
      .claude/settings.json — committed to the repo and shared with the whole team. Whatever you set here becomes the team baseline. A personal “allow” at User or Local cannot soften it.
    
    
    
    Merge rule
    
    
    deny > ask > allow. For any permission pattern, Claude Code takes the strictest bucket it finds across all three files. Setting “inherit” at a layer means that layer emits no rule — Claude Code falls back to its built-in default for that pattern.
    
    
    Dashboard convention
    
    
    Presets set the Local layer — your personal default for this project. The User layer is for cross-project preferences; the Project layer is for team-wide policy. Rows set to “inherit” are omitted from the emitted YAML.
    
  
    

    


    
  Generated by scripts/generate-dashboards.py.
  Source schema: plugins/ravenclaude-core/dashboard-schema.json.
  Design: proposal 003.
